title: Tennessee SB 1236 Fails to Abide By its Own Preamble
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman criticizes Tennessee SB1236 for asking the Supreme Court's permission to make abortion only a Class C felony, arguing the bill enshrines judicial idolatry, denies preborn children equal protection under the 14th Amendment, and contradicts its own preamble by failing to immediately and totally abolish abortion.
audiences: Tennessee pro-life voter weighing whether to support SB1236
State legislator drafting an abortion bill
Christian pastor wrestling with civil obedience and the lesser magistrate
Pro-life activist debating incrementalism vs. immediatism

questions.question: What does the author say SB1236 actually accomplishes, as opposed to what its supporters claim?
questions.audience: Tennessee pro-life voter weighing whether to support SB1236
questions.answer: Silberman argues the bill is a substitute for real action. Rather than abolishing abortion, it begs the Supreme Court for permission to act and downgrades the murder of a preborn child to a Class C felony, which he says further dehumanizes those children and entrenches judicial supremacy.
questions.quote: This bill accomplishes nothing but entrench the judicial idolatry that is perpetuating the murder and further dehumanize preborn children by treating their murder as only a Class C felony.

questions.question: Why does the author label Section 1(a) of the bill 'judicial idolatry'?
questions.audience: State legislator drafting an abortion bill
questions.answer: Because that section conditions the entire bill on the Supreme Court's approval, the author says the drafters effectively concede that Tennessee lacks authority to protect life if the Court disagrees. He calls this constitutionally and morally illiterate cowardice and insists states have both the authority and the obligation to defy the Court when it sanctions mass murder.
questions.quote: Section 1 (a) of the bill explicitly subjects the bill to judicial tyranny, establishing that the authors of the bill do not believe the state of Tennessee has the authority to uphold the unalienable right to life if the Supreme Court opines that they shouldn't.

questions.question: How does treating abortion as a Class C felony violate equal protection in the author's view?
questions.answer: Silberman argues that assigning a lesser penalty to the killing of preborn humans than to the killing of born humans creates an unequal class of personhood, violating the 14th Amendment's equal protection clause and committing the sin of partiality. He says it dehumanizes preborn boys and girls by codifying that their lives merit less legal protection.
questions.quote: By providing preborn human beings a lesser level of protection than born human beings, the authors of the bill have dehumanized preborn boys and girls, written an unjust law, committed the sin of partiality, violated the 14th amendment which prohibits unequal protection, and have entirely undermined the case against abortion.

questions.question: How does the author use Roe v. Wade itself to argue against unequal penalties?
questions.audience: Pro-life activist debating incrementalism vs. immediatism
questions.answer: He points to Justice Blackmun's footnote 54, which exploited the fact that Texas treated abortion with lighter penalties than murder and exempted the woman from prosecution. Silberman says this gave the Court a hook to deny preborn personhood, and he warns that SB1236 repeats the same self-defeating mistake that helped produce Roe.
questions.quote: That the state of Texas did not provide equal protection for preborn human beings by treating the murder of a preborn human being as murder betrayed the fact that even the pro-lifers didn't think the preborn child was a human being.

questions.question: What does the author mean when he says SB1236's authors must 'abide by their own preamble'?
questions.audience: Christian pastor wrestling with civil obedience and the lesser magistrate
questions.answer: The bill's preamble cites the 14th Amendment's prohibition on depriving any person of life or denying equal protection. Silberman argues that if the legislators actually believe their own preamble, they are bound to refuse enforcement of any law, court opinion, or regulation, including pro-life regulations and Roe itself, that lets preborn people be killed.
questions.quote: The authors of Tennessee SB1236 must abide by their own preamble and refuse to enforce any law, court opinion, or regulation which deprives preborn human beings of life.

questions.question: What action does the author say Tennessee should take instead of passing SB1236?
questions.audience: State legislator drafting an abortion bill
questions.answer: He calls for the state to ignore Roe outright and pass total, immediate abolition rather than seeking judicial permission or settling for partial penalties. Anything short of that, he argues, is itself unconstitutional under the 14th Amendment standard the bill's own preamble invokes.
questions.quote: They must disregard Roe as the moral atrocity and legal nullity that it is and immediately and totally abolish abortion, thereby upholding the unalienable right to life.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Lays out the immediatist case against incremental bills like SB1236.

related.title: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
related.reason: Parallel case study of pro-life politicians blocking equal protection legislation in another state.

related.title: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
related.reason: Develops the same 'judicial idolatry' critique Silberman levels at SB1236's Section 1(a).

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the 'Villains' of American History?
related.reason: Defends a state's authority and duty to defy unjust federal court rulings, the remedy SB1236 refuses to apply.

related.title: Pro-Life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence to 110,000 Preborn Human Beings
related.reason: Another example of a pro-life legislator killing an equal protection abolition bill, showing the same pattern Silberman attacks.

related.title: Don't Fall For Texas Right to Life's Faux Abolitionism
related.reason: Explains how regulatory bills get packaged as abolition, the rhetorical move SB1236's preamble makes.

related.title: Abortion Abolition Bill Introduced in Washington
related.reason: Contrasting example of a true abolition bill that does what Silberman demands of Tennessee.
